coredata.dc:title: Aminophenol functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent sensor for nitroalkenes
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Shengchen
Wang, Peng
Liu, Donghui
Liu, Xueke
Zhou, Zhiqiang
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: To set up effective analysis method is important for environmental monitoring. In this work, the 4-aminophenol functionalized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (AP-NSCDs) were synthesized based on the functionalization of NSCDs obtained by one-step hydrothermal method and used as a new fluorescent sensor for the determination of nitroalkene pesticide and medicine, such as nitenpyram, nizatidine and ranitidine. Nitroalkenes could quench the fluorescence of the functionalized carbon dots due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle. The sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, with a wide linear range of 0.05 ∼ 10 mg/L and a low detection limit of 0.02 mg/L. This sensor was successfully employed to detect nitenpyram in rice, orange and cabbage, and to determine nizatidine and ranitidine in serum, urine and river water as well. The developed method was rapid and sensitive, and had potential for monitoring the nitroalkene compounds in the environment and food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aminophenol functionalized carbon quantum dots
Nitroalkenes
Fluorescent sensor
Pesticide
Medicine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The interaction mechanism of the nitenpyram with AP-NSCDs
The application of the AP-NSCDs sensor for the determination of the nitroalkenes in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the 2115 Talent Development Program of China Agricultural University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B), size distribution image(C) and (D) of AP-NSCDs.
XPS spectrum of AP-NSCDs. full scan (A), high-resolution of C 1s (B), high-resolution of N 1s (C) and high-resolution of S 2p (D).
Fluorescence emission spectrum of AP-NSCDs in the presence of nitenpyram (from 0.05 to 10 mg/L) in acetonitrile (A) and the standard curve of nitenpyram (B), nitenpyram (C) and ranitidine (D).
Time scan of AP-NSCDs and AP-NSCDs with nitenpyram (NIT) (A) and emission spectra of AP-NSCDs in acetonitrile and water and absorption spectra of nitenpyram (B).
Determinations of nitenpyram, nizatidine and ranitidine in samples by the fluorescent sensor.

head.ce:title: Aminophenol functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent sensor for nitroalkenes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, No. 2, West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, No. 2, West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To set up effective analysis method is important for environmental monitoring. In this work, the 4-aminophenol functionalized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (AP-NSCDs) were synthesized based on the functionalization of NSCDs obtained by one-step hydrothermal method and used as a new fluorescent sensor for the determination of nitroalkene pesticide and medicine, such as nitenpyram, nizatidine and ranitidine. Nitroalkenes could quench the fluorescence of the functionalized carbon dots due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle. The sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, with a wide linear range of 0.05 ∼ 10 mg/L and a low detection limit of 0.02 mg/L. This sensor was successfully employed to detect nitenpyram in rice, orange and cabbage, and to determine nizatidine and ranitidine in serum, urine and river water as well. The developed method was rapid and sensitive, and had potential for monitoring the nitroalkene compounds in the environment and food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Medicine and pesticide are important chemicals that are often used in human activities. However, the large-scale use of these substances creates environmental residual problems and poses threat to the environmental organisms and human. It was found that the concentration of pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) represented by oral contraceptives and anti-inflammatory medicines in the water of the Yangtze River of China in 2021 ranging from 412.55 ng/L to 5399.85 ng/L [1]. Imidacloprid was determined at the highest concentration of 13 μg/kg in streams near agricultural production greenhouses of Swedish in 2018 [2]. Some compounds could be enriched in some biological or environmental matrices due to their persistence and bioaccumulation. A study showed that the concentration of p,p′-DDE in the zooplankton of the Sacramento River of USA in 2019 was 139.2 ng/g, but in the salmon was 1060 ng/g [3].

To analyze these compounds rapidly and accurately is very important for environmental safety, food safety and human health. Some large-sized instrumental methods are already in use, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, gas chromatography and capillary electrophoresis. These methods have good separation ability, accuracy and precision. However, their disadvantages of cumbersome operation, complex sample pretreatment, and high cost of use cannot be ignored. From a complementary view, people have developed rapid detection methods. Among them, the fluorescence method is easy to operate, and the signal is visible and the response is fast [4]. Chen et al synthesized UiO-66-NH@TpTG@TB fluorescent material, which interacted with the polar antibiotic norfloxacin through the positive charge on the surface, achieving a sensitive detection limit of 4 nM [5]. In addition, the whole detection process can be completed within 50 s. At present, there are still some problems to be solved in the fluorescence analysis method. On the one hand, most analytes cannot produce fluorescence by themselves. If derivatization reaction is involved to produce fluorescence the analysis time will be prolonged, which may not meet the need of rapid detection [6]. On the other hand, fluorescence can be disturbed by other substances, such as metal ions and biological proteins [7,8]. Developing a specific fluorescent sensor is a major and challenging task in fluorescence analysis method.

Carbon nano fluorescent materials, represented by carbon quantum dots (CDs), have attracted much attention because of their low toxicity, abundant precursors, easy synthesis and excellent optical properties [9–13]. It is used in many fields such as biological imaging, catalysis and sensors [14–16]. In recent years, CDs materials with green raw materials [17–19], high selectivity and sensitivity [20–22] have been widely studied.

Doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur and boron can improve the optical properties such as quantum yield and emission wavelength by changing the energy band gap and electron density [23]. Meanwhile, the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl and other functional groups on the surface of CDs also provide good sites for further modifications. For a rapid detection method, the surface functional groups of quantum dots cannot provide enough selectivity. Therefore, connecting CDs with enzymes, aptamers and molecularly imprinted polymers can improve the selectivity of the sensor [24,25].

Wang et al prepared 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid functionalized nitrogen-CDs, which could be used for gentamicin with detection limit (LOD) of 0.29 × 10–4 mol/L [26]. At present, hydrothermal method has been preferred to prepare CDs. and achieve the purpose of modification. Covalent modifications have also been widely studied linking specific recognition elements with CDs through amidation reactions, esterification reactions and silylation reactions to improve the specificity of detection [27].

Nitroalkenes have a nitro structure with a strong electron-absorbing ability that is highly electronically attractive as an acceptor. It is capable of acting as a medicine or pesticide. For example, nitenpyram is one of the widely used neonicotinoid pesticides, which can interact with insect nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChR). The results of crystal diffraction showed that the hydrogen bond with amino acid residues played a key role [28]. The nitroalkene compounds nizatidine and ranitidine are gastric medicines that can inhibit gastric acid secretion, in which the hydrogen bond interaction between nitroalkenes and various amino acid residues also plays a key role [29]. Based on this relationship, we designed the modification of 4-aminophenol groups on the surface of CDs, intending to determine the nitroalkenes by interacting with phenolic hydroxyl groups on the CDs and the induced fluorescence quenching.

As far as we know, there are few rapid detection methods for nitroalkenes. In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method, and 4-aminophenol group was modified on the surface of NSCDs by amidation reaction to obtain fluorescent material. The rapid analysis method for nitroalkene compounds such as nitenpyram, nizatidine and ranitidine based on this fluorescent sensor has been set up. The application of the developed methods for the analytes in real samples was investigated. The purpose of this work is to supply a powerful and efficient tool for rapid detection of the nitroalkene pesticide and medicine residues.

All analytical grade reagents used were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without further purification, unless indicated otherwise. Dialysis bag (MW:3500) was obtained from Beijing Labgic Technology Co. Ltd. Nitenpyram was provided by Nanjing Red Sun Co. Ltd with purity over 98%. Orange, rice and cabbage were purchased from local super market in Beijing, China. Serum and urine were from mouse supplied by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co. Ltd. The river water sample was taken from Wanquan River (Beijing, China). All samples did not contain analytes. Deionized water (resistance > 18.2 MΩ) was used for all experiments.

Excitation and emission spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan). A TU-1810PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Persee, China) was used to record absorption spectra and measure absorbance. A Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN (FEI, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV was applied to characterize the morphology and size of AP-NSCDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were recorded on a ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra were conducted using a IRtrace-100 (Shimadzu, Japan), scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1. X-ray diffraction analysis using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany) instrument. Fluorescence lifetime was measured using FLS1000 (Edinburgh, UK) transient fluorescence spectrometer.

NSCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to reference [30]: Briefly, 1 g of l-cysteine and 2 g of citric acid were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water and reacted at 70 °C for 12 h. The intermediate product was subsequently transferred into a 50-mL hydrothermal reactor and the reaction was kept at 200 °C for 3 h. The product was adjusted to pH 7 with 1 mol/L NaOH and purified in a dialysis bag for 72 h (MW:3500). The dialysate was diluted to 300 mL (10 mg/mL) and stored in the dark for future use.

AP-NSCDs were prepared by modifying NSCDs with amidation reaction. N-hydroxy succinimide (50 mg) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (50 mg) were used as catalysts to mix with the NSCDs (10 mg/mL, 3 mL) solution evenly. Then, the above solution was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water in a flask with the stirring. The carboxyl group was activated by stirring for 15 min after sealing. Then, 100 mg of 4-aminophenol was added. The flask was sealed and stirred for 12 h. The product was purified in a dialysis bag for 24 h (MW:3500) and stored in the dark for future use.

Fluorescence emission spectrum (370 nm–500 nm) was performed at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm, with a slit width of 5 nm for excitation and emission wavelength. In the analytical procedure, 100 μL of AP-NSCDs (1 mg/mL) was added into 880 μL of acetonitrile. Then, 20 μL of nitenpyram, nizatidine or ranitidine acetonitrile solutions were added into above solution, and the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm was taken. All fluorescence measurements were performed with at least three parallels.

In order to evaluate the ability of fluorescence sensor to distinguish between target and interference, structural analogues of nitroalkenes including cimetidine, acetamiprid, thiamethoxam and imidacloprid, possible interfering compounds such as fipronil, chlorpyrifos, methomyl, carbendazim, triadimenol, glyphosate and atrazine, as well as cation (Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+), anion (SO4 2−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, Cl−, F−) were explored as coexisting substances. The concentration of all interfering substances was 100 mg/L.

The application of the analytical procedure for nitenpyram in agricultural products such as rice, orange and cabbage were investigated. The samples were homogenized or smashed, and nitenpyram was added. Then the sample was allowed to stand for 30 min. The spiked concentration was 0.5, 1, 5 mg/kg in rice and orange, and 0.2, 1, 2 mg/kg in cabbage. Pretreatments were needed before the determination. For rice, take 2 g of the sample and add 2 mL of acetonitrile. Then, the mixture was vortexed for 5 min, and the supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for further determination. For orange and cabbage, take 2 g of the sample and add 2 mL of acetonitrile and 0.6 g of NaCl. The mixture was stirred for 5 min on a vortex mixer, and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was collected and added with 20 mg of PSA and 20 mg of C18 for cleanup. In particular, 20 μL of 0.01 g/L copper sulfate solution were added for the cleanup of cabbage sample to reduce the fluorescence interference produced by plant pigments. The supernatant was centrifuged again for further determination.

For nizatidine and ranitidine, serum, urine and river water were selected as real samples. Nizatidine was spiked in each sample at three levels: 0.1, 0.5 and 1 mg/L, and ranitidine was spiked in each sample at 0.5, 1 and 5 mg/L. Take 2 mL of the sample and add 2 mL of acetonitrile and 0.6 g of NaCl. The mixture was stirred for 5 min on a vortex mixer, and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was collected and cleaned with 20 mg of PSA and 20 mg of C18, and centrifuged again.

For the determination, mix 900 μL of the above extracting solution with 100 μL of AP-NSCDs (1 mg/mL), and the fluorescence detection was performed as described in Section 2.5 and the absorbance were recorded for quantification.

The NSCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid and l-cysteine, and the functionalization of 4-aminophenol group was realized by amidation reaction. The AP-NSCDs had excellent solubility in water. The microstructure and morphology identified by the TEM image and XRD were shown in Fig. 1 . AP-NSCDs were not uniform in shape and their size were about 2.0–5.2 nm, with an average diameter of 3.3 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) suggested that most AP-NSCDs possessed a crystalline core with an amorphous surface. The lattice spacing was about 0.21 nm, which was similar to graphite, which proved that its carbon atoms were sp2 hybrid. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of AP-NSCDs showed a broad peak with a center of 23.9°, which belonged to their graphite structure. This was consistent with the information provided by HRTEM.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to further study the functional groups and molecular structure of NSCDs and AP-NSCDs. In Fig. S9 A, the broad absorption band around 3410 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H, N-H. This showed that the surface of NSCDs was rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, which was conducive to increasing hydrophilicity. The peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 3180 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorption of C=C and C=C–H. This was the main conjugated structure of NSCDs and produced fluorescence. The peaks at 1679 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorption of C=O and C–N. Meanwhile, weak C=S and C–S signals were observed at 1203 cm−1 and 609 cm−1. For the FT-IR (Fig. S9 B) of AP-NSCDs, the most obvious features were the amide Ⅰ peak and amide Ⅱ peak at 1654 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1. This indicated that 4-aminophenol was successfully attached to the carboxyl group on the surface of the NSCDs through the amidation reaction. In addition, the vibration of the active hydrogen at 3313 cm−1 also became sharper which indicated the presence of phenolic hydroxyl groups. The full-scan XPS spectrum of AP-NSCDs was shown in Fig. 2 . Except for three peaks at 531, 400 and 285 eV, corresponding to O 1s, N 1s and C 1s, respectively, there were two peaks at 232 and 167, which caused by S 1s and S 2p. High resolution scans of three different peaks in the C 1s region indicated that the carbon was in three different chemical states. The peak near 284.6 eV indicated the presence of sp2 (C=C) graphitized structure in AP-NSCDs. The peaks near 285.8 eV can be attributed to sp3 bonded carbon with oxygen (C–O), sulfur (C–S) and nitrogen (C–N), while the peak centered at 287.5 eV can be attributed to C=O. The N 1s spectrum showed two peaks, centered at 399.7 and 399.1 eV, for amide bond (C–N) and pyridine (C–N–C). High-resolution scans of S 2p showed sulfur in three different chemical states, S–C and S–O represented at 163.4 eV and 167.4 eV and the thiophene structure at 165.5 eV.

Zeta potential can reflect the stability of the system and the functional group information on the surface of carbon dots. According to Table S3, the Zeta potential of the functionalized carbon dots increased from −24.2 mV to −9.4 mV. This indicates that the carboxyl group on the surface of the AP-NSCDs was replaced by a large number of phenol groups that are not easy to ionize.
AP-NSCDs solution presented a transparent tan color in sunlight, while showed bright blue fluorescence under emission of 419 nm with UV lamp. The maximum excitation wavelength was 345 nm.

Under optimized conditions, the response ability of AP-NSCDs to nitenpyram was studied. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of AP-NSCDs decreased apparently with the increase of nitenpyram concentration (0.05–10 mg/L) in a linear relationship. Nitroalkenes in the range of 0.01 to 50 mg/L were tested to evaluate the analytical performance of the probe. The fluorescence quenching by nitenpyram was in the linear range of 0.05–10 mg/L (Fig. 3), which can fit the equation (F − F0) = 347.51 C − 1.94 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.997. In this equation, C is the concentration of nitenpyram. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of AP-NSCDs in the presence and absence of nitenpyram. When the signal to noise ratio was 3, the LOD was 0.02 mg/L. For ranitidine the linear range was 0.05–10 mg/L, and the LOD was 0.02 mg/L. The equation was (F − F0) = 373.26 C + 40.531 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.997. For nizatidine, the linear range was 0.03–10 mg/L, and the LOD was 0.01 mg/L. The equation was (F − F0) = 433.16 C − 7.035 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.998.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of AP-NSCDs to nitroalkenes, the fluorescence intensity under the interference of structural analogues of nitroalkenes, possible interfering compounds and ions was investigated. Fig. 4 showed none of the substances at the tested concentration of 100 mg/L had significant interfering effects (variations in fluorescence intensity of more than 5%) on the fluorescence intensity, except Fe3+ had slight interference (16%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction mechanism of the nitenpyram with AP-NSCDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis, FTIR and fluorescence lifetimes analysis were used to discuss the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of the AP-NSCDs to nitenpyram. The FT-IR information (Fig. S9 C) showed that the absorption peak attributed to the hydroxyl group of phenol at 3500 cm−1 was significantly weakened after adding nitenpyram to the AP-NSCDs, indicating the phenolic hydroxyl group on the surface of AP-NSCDs was involved in the recognition process.

The fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 4 A) showed that there was no significant difference between the fluorescence lifetimes of 6.53 ns and 6.23 ns before and after the addition of nitenpyram, respectively, which proved that the system was statically quenched.

In addition, the overlap between the UV absorption of nitenpyram and emission spectra of AP-NSCDs suggested that nitenpyram might absorb the light generated by AP-NSCDs, which quenched the fluorescence through an energy resonance transfer mechanism. Acetonitrile causes a blue shift in the emission wavelength of AP-NSCDs, increasing the overlap area and quenching effectiveness (Fig. 4 B).

Putting all the information together, the interaction between the nitenpyram and the hydroxyl group of the phenol group on the surface of the AP-NSCDs could result in the transfer of electrons on the phenolic hydroxyl oxygen that were originally conjugated to the benzene ring and thus caused the fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of the AP-NSCDs sensor for the determination of the nitroalkenes in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the method was tested by recovery. At listed in Table 1 , the obtained recoveries varied from 81% to 96%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was below 9.3%. According to Chinese national standards, the maximum residue limits (MRL) of nitenpyram is between 0.2 and 1 mg/kg. Therefore, the fluorescent probe proposed in this paper has sufficient sensitivity to detect the content of nitenpyram in the rice, orange and cabbage. The method had high reproducibility and precision. It provided a good alternative method for the rapid detection of nitenpyram in relevant food samples and the detection of nizatidine and ranitidine in biological samples and water.

An AP-NSCD fluorescent probe was developed based on aminophenol functionalization using citric acid, l-cysteine and 4-aminophenol as raw materials, which had good stability and water solubility. The fluorescent responding specificity to nitroalkenes made it applicable to determine the nitroalkene pesticide and medicine such as nitenpyram, nizatidine, and ranitidine with good performance. The possible mechanism could be the transfer of electrons because of the hydrogen bond interaction between the nitro group of nitroalkene structure and the phenolic hydroxyl group, which caused the fluorescence quenching of AP-NSCD. The proposed method could be applied for the monitoring of the nitroalkenes in real samples such as rice, orange, cabbage, serum, urine and river water, which was simple, rapid, sensitive, and environmentally friendly.

Shengchen Lv: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Peng Wang: Validation. Donghui Liu: Funding acquisition, Resources. Xueke Liu: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Zhiqiang Zhou: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 2115 Talent Development Program of China Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108569.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
